The 787 has larger windows, better cabin pressure and humidity than older aircraft — less dehydrating on long haul.

Time zone: Seoul is UTC+9. London (BST) is UTC+1 in April — 8 hours ahead.
Tip: Set watches to Seoul time as you board and try to sleep — arrival Day 1 has a full afternoon planned.

items.notes: Evening street stalls — tteokbokki, hotteok, Korean fried chicken. K-beauty shops.
items.address: Myeongdong Street, Jung-gu, Seoul 04534, South Korea
items.detail:
Myeongdong at night is the classic Seoul evening. Street stalls fill the pedestrian street from ~17:00.

Must try:
- Tteokbokki (spicy rice cake skewers) — the defining Seoul street food
- Hotteok (warm sugar-filled pancake)
- Korean fried chicken — lighter and crispier than Western versions
- Tornado potato on a stick
- Egg bread (gyeran ppang) — warm, savoury, filling

K-beauty: Innisfree, Etude House, Laneige, Sulwhasoo, Olive Young. Good for gifts.
Payment: Most stalls and shops accept card. Some stalls cash only.

items.notes: Samgyeopsal (pork belly) or galbi (beef ribs). ₩60,000–80,000 for 4.
items.address: Myeongdong Street, Jung-gu, Seoul 04534, South Korea
items.detail:
Korean BBQ — meat grilled at the table, eaten wrapped in lettuce with garlic, gochujang and banchan (side dishes).

Best cuts for first-timers:
- Samgyeopsal (삼겹살): Thick-cut pork belly — juicy, fatty, the default
- Chadolbaegi (차돌박이): Very thinly sliced beef brisket — cooks in seconds
- Galbi (갈비): Marinated beef short ribs — sweet, tender

How it works: Order meat by the portion. Staff usually cook the first batch. Banchan (side dishes) are refillable free — always ask for more.
Drinks: Soju (₩5,000/bottle) or somaek (beer + soju mixed).

ideas.title: Deoksugung Palace — Changing of the Guard
ideas.notes: Compact, beautiful royal palace. 20 min walk from Crown Park. Guard ceremony at 2pm most days. ₩1,000pp. Allow 1 hour.

ideas.title: N Seoul Tower — Namsan cable car
ideas.notes: Cable car ₩9,500pp return · Tower entry ₩21,000pp. Panoramic city views. Best at sunset but gets busy.

ideas.title: Bukchon Hanok Village
ideas.notes: Traditional Korean village, wooden hanok houses. Best in late afternoon light. Taxi 15 min from hotel. Free to explore.

ideas.title: Korean BBQ dinner in Myeongdong
ideas.notes: Samgyeopsal (pork belly) or galbi (beef ribs). ₩60,000–80,000 for 4. Many excellent spots within walking distance of Crown Park.

ideas.title: 7-Eleven ATM — get Korean won
ideas.notes: Don't exchange at the airport. 7-Eleven ATMs accept UK cards and give much better rates.

items.notes: 2 min walk from Keio Plaza. Narrow alleyway of tiny yakitori bars. Cash only.
items.address: Omoide Yokocho, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo 160-0021, Japan
items.detail:
Omoide Yokocho (思い出横丁) — Memory Lane
Distance: 2 min walk northwest of Shinjuku Station West Exit
Hours: Most bars open from 17:00 until midnight+
⚠ Cash only in almost all bars

One of Tokyo's most atmospheric spots — barely 2m wide, tiny bars seating 6–8 people each, filled with charcoal smoke and grilling chicken. Looks exactly as it did in the 1960s.

How it works: Walk in, pick a bar with seats. Point at what you want. Best skewers: Negima (chicken and spring onion), tsukune (meatball), kawa (crispy skin), torikawa (cartilage). Usually ¥150–300 each.

Tip: 'Kore kudasai' (this please) + pointing works perfectly. The atmosphere is the whole point on night one.

days.label: Day 3 — Monday 6 Apr
days.locationAddress: Keio Plaza Hotel Tokyo, 2-2-1 Nishishinjuku, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo 160-8330, Japan

items.notes: Free breakfast included.
items.address: Keio Plaza Hotel Tokyo, 2-2-1 Nishishinjuku, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo 160-8330, Japan
items.detail:
Keio Plaza Hotel breakfast — included in room rate.
Venue: Main dining room, floors 3–4. Hours: 07:00–10:00 (busy 08:00–09:00 — go early or late).
Japanese breakfast: Miso soup, grilled salmon, tamagoyaki, natto, rice, pickles. Great introduction to Japanese breakfast culture.
Tip: Load up — full day of walking ahead.

items.notes: JR Yamanote Line from Shinjuku. Takeshita Street · Meiji Shrine · Shibuya Crossing.
items.address: Shibuya Crossing, 2-2-1 Dogenzaka, Shibuya-ku, Tokyo 150-0043, Japan
items.detail:
Getting there: JR Yamanote Line from Shinjuku (1 stop to Harajuku, 2 to Shibuya). ~4 min. Use Suica.

Harajuku (start here):
- Takeshita Street: Famous youth fashion street. Narrow, colourful, chaotic. Crepe shops, kawaii fashion.
- Omotesando: Wide tree-lined luxury boulevard 10 min south. Flagship stores, beautiful architecture.
- Meiji Shrine: Enormous forested Shinto shrine. 10 min walk from Takeshita St. Free. Allow 45 mins.

Shibuya Crossing:
- 3,000 people crossing simultaneously every 60 seconds. Best viewed from 2F Starbucks or Mag's Park.
- Shibuya Scramble Square 12F: Free secret viewpoint behind the teppanyaki restaurant.
- Parco Shibuya: Floor 6 = Nintendo Tokyo, Pokémon Center Shibuya. Queues 30–60 mins — go early.

items.notes: Oldest Tokyo temple (645 AD). Kaminarimon Gate, side streets to beat crowds. Free entry.
items.address: Senso-ji Temple, 2-3-1 Asakusa, Taito-ku, Tokyo 111-0032, Japan
items.detail:
Senso-ji Temple (浅草寺) — Asakusa
Access: Tokyo Metro Ginza Line from Shibuya → Asakusa (30 min, ¥260). Entry: Free

Kaminarimon Gate: The enormous red lantern gate is iconic. Nakamise-dori (souvenir street) leads to the main hall.
The main hall: Wave incense smoke over yourself for good health. ¥5 coin for a prayer.
Omikuji: Fortune papers ¥100 — if the fortune is bad, tie it to the wire rack.

Beat the crowds: Walk left or right of main Nakamise into the side alleys — same temple, fraction of the tourists. The five-storey pagoda is particularly peaceful.
Nearby: Asakusa Culture Tourist Information Centre (opposite main gate) — free 8th floor observation deck with great views.

items.notes: Neon-lit entertainment district. Ramen, yakitori, izakaya.
items.address: Shinjuku Station, 3-38-1 Shinjuku, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo 160-0022, Japan
items.detail:
Kabukicho & East Shinjuku — Dinner Options

Kabukicho is safe, lively and fun. Top options nearby:
- Ramen: Ichiran (solo booths, ¥900–1,200pp) or Fuunji (tsukemen — thick dipping ramen, queue outside ~20 min)
- Gyukatsu Motomura: Lightly fried beef cutlets, cook on a small hot stone at the table. ~¥1,500pp
- Torikizoku: Excellent-value yakitori chain — everything ¥385 per item. Loud, fun, very local

After dinner: Golden Gai is 5 min walk — themed tiny bars, cash only, cover charge ¥500–1,000.

items.notes: Six alleys of tiny themed bars — 200 venues. Cash only in most bars.
items.address: Golden Gai, Kabukicho, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo 160-0021, Japan
items.detail:
Golden Gai (ゴールデン街)
Location: Off Hanazono Shrine, east of Kabukicho — 12 min walk from Keio Plaza
Hours: Most bars 19:00 until late · ⚠ Cash only · Cover charge ¥500–1,000 in many bars

~200 tiny bars across 6 narrow alleyways. Each bar seats 4–12 people, each has its own theme — film, jazz, horror, art, manga. Some for regulars only (English sign if so). Most welcome tourists.

How it works: Walk the alleys, find a bar that appeals, check cover charge sign, sit down. Drinks ¥800–1,500 each.
Best approach: No plan — the discovery is the point. Don't like a bar? Finish your drink and move on.
Nearby: Hanazono Shrine is right next to Golden Gai — lantern-lit at night, free, always open.

days.title: Tokyo — Shibuya · Harajuku · Asakusa

ideas.title: Shibuya Sky rooftop viewpoint
ideas.notes: Open-air observation deck on Shibuya Scramble Square. 360° Tokyo views. Book midnight 30 days in advance.

ideas.title: Shibuya 12F hidden viewpoint (free)
ideas.notes: Free secret viewing area behind the teppanyaki restaurant on the 12th floor of Shibuya Scramble Square. No one talks about it.

ideas.title: Harajuku thrift: Laforet / Loveless / Atmos / Beauty & Youth
ideas.notes: Best Harajuku stores for youth fashion, streetwear and mid-range basics. Dense — allow 2 hours minimum.

ideas.title: 2nd Street / Mode Off / Treasure Factory (thrift chains)
ideas.notes: Large-format vintage and second-hand chains. Great for clothing, records, accessories. Very local experience.

ideas.title: Don Quijote Shibuya
ideas.notes: Largest Don Quijote in Japan. Open 24/7. Less crowded after 10pm. Classic Japan experience — everything under one roof.

ideas.title: Omoide Yokocho (Memory Lane) — yakitori alley
ideas.notes: 2 min walk from Keio Plaza. Tiny smoky yakitori bars. Cash only. Classic Tokyo first evening.

ideas.title: Tir na nÓg underground bar, Ginza
ideas.notes: Underground cocktail bar. Cover charge likely ¥500–1,000pp. Check sign at entrance.

ideas.title: [JACK] Parco Shibuya
ideas.notes: 10-floor mall near Shibuya crossing — young, hip, cultural hub. Floor 6 is the draw: Nintendo Tokyo (Japan's first), Pokémon Center Shibuya, Capcom Store, SEGA Store, Godzilla Store. Serious queues on floor 6 — go early or expect 30–60 min waits. Floors 1–5 have excellent fashion (Acne Studios, APC, Brain Dead, AMBUSH, OUR LEGACY). Chaos Kitchen basement = winding alleyway food court. Rooftop terrace on floor 10. 5 min walk from Shibuya Station Hachiko exit.

ideas.title: [JACK] Super Bookoff / Bookoff Super Bazaar
ideas.notes: Japan's biggest second-hand chain with 850+ stores. The Super Bazaar format is enormous — books, manga, CDs, retro video games, vintage clothing, instruments, branded goods, designer bags (authenticated). All items are cleaned and graded. Tax-free with passport on purchases over ¥5,000. Best Tokyo branches: Shibuya (fashion-forward), Shimokitazawa (curated vintage), Akihabara (6 floors, retro games and manga). Good for Japanese-label vintage and cheap CDs.

ideas.title: [JACK] 2nd Street
ideas.notes: Japan-wide chain of second-hand stores. Stock varies dramatically by neighbourhood. Harajuku/Shimokitazawa branches are best for vintage fashion — good for Japanese-label pieces, Americana, Levi's, workwear. Downstairs section at Harajuku branch is particularly strong. Also in Osaka (Amerikamura area). Prices mid-range — not thrift-cheap but fair for what you find.

ideas.title: [JACK] Amam Dacotan — artisan bakery & café
ideas.notes: Omotesando / Kita-Aoyama. One of Tokyo's most famous and talked-about bakeries — extraordinary bread, sandwiches and sweets. Two locations close together: main bakery at 3-7-6 Kita-Aoyama; café & bake at 3-6-23 Kita-Aoyama (1F). Both a few minutes from Omotesando Station Exit B2. Always a queue — arrive early. Perfect combination with Harajuku/Omotesando day.

ideas.title: [JACK] Pain des Philosophes — croissants
ideas.notes: Kagurazaka, Shinjuku City. Selected Tabelog Top 100 bakery 2022. Consistently called the best croissant in Tokyo — proper French technique, buttery and flaky with Japanese touches. Signature furikake-filled baguette is unusual and excellent. Pain au chocolat and Asama loaf also outstanding. Opens 9am, always has a queue, often sells out by noon. Tiny shop — go early, eat in the nearby park. 107-2 Yaraicho, Shinjuku City.
ideas.mapQuery: Pain des Philosophes Kagurazaka Shinjuku Tokyo

ideas.title: [JACK] Laitier — soft serve & desserts
ideas.notes: Sendagaya, Shibuya-ku. French-inspired dessert café — premium soft serve ice cream and sweets. 1-22-7 Sendagaya 1F. Near Sendagaya Station — easy detour between Harajuku and Shibuya.

ideas.title: [JACK] Nephew — café-bar, carrot cake
ideas.notes: Tomigaya, Shibuya-ku — the cool, local Yoyogi-Hachiman / Yoyogi-Koen neighbourhood. Tiny house-style bar and sweets café. 1-7-2 Tomigaya. Jack specifically recommends for the carrot cake. Very local, off the tourist map — good escape from main strips.

ideas.title: [JACK] Poem Mano a Mano Coffee — fruit sandwiches
ideas.notes: Koenji, Suginami — a few minutes from Koenji Station on the Chuo Line. Classic old-school kissaten (Japanese coffee shop) style café. Famous for exceptional filter coffee, French toast and especially fruit sandwiches — seasonal fruit (Shine Muscat, mango, others) in soft shokupan with generous fresh cream. Regularly highlighted as a must-order. Koenji itself is the best thrift/vintage neighbourhood in Tokyo — pair this with a wander through the vintage shops and backstreets. Very local, retro, indie atmosphere.
ideas.mapQuery: Poem Mano a Mano Coffee Koenji Tokyo

days.label: Day 4 — Tuesday 7 Apr
days.locationAddress: Keio Plaza Hotel Tokyo, 2-2-1 Nishishinjuku, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo 160-8330, Japan

items.notes: Early start — fuel up before Sumo.
items.address: Keio Plaza Hotel Tokyo, 2-2-1 Nishishinjuku, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo 160-8330, Japan

items.notes: Free walk-up viewing through street-facing window. No reservation needed. 7:30–10:00am. Arrive by 6:45am in April — gets crowded fast. Watch wrestlers train from outside (window has mesh so photography is tricky — enjoy in person rather than worrying about shots). Wrestlers sometimes come outside for photos after practice. No talking, no flash, no food/drink. 2-47-2 Nihonbashihamacho, Chuo-ku — 2 min from Hamacho Station Exit A2 (Toei Shinjuku Line). Check calendar before going: arashio.net/tour_e.html
items.address: Keio Plaza Hotel Tokyo, 2-2-1 Nishishinjuku, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo 160-8330, Japan

items.notes: Tamagoyaki, uni, grilled scallops. Go stall to stall — most sell out by late morning.
items.address: Tsukiji Outer Market, 4-16-2 Tsukiji, Chuo-ku, Tokyo 104-0045, Japan
items.detail:
Tsukiji Outer Market (築地場外市場)
Access: From Hamacho after sumo, Toei Shinjuku Line to Shintomicho (~10 min) then 10 min walk. Or taxi ~¥700.
Hours: Most stalls 05:00–14:00. Go early for best selection.

Must try while walking:
- Tamagoyaki (sweet rolled omelette) on a stick — buy fresh from Tsukumo or Maruya
- Uni (sea urchin) on a rice bowl — ¥1,500–2,000 for a small, rich bowl
- Grilled scallops (hotate) with butter and soy — ¥500–800 each
- Tamago sushi (egg nigiri) — simple and excellent here

Souvenirs: Nori, bonito flakes, wasabi, kitchen knives (world-class), dashi stock packs.
Knives: Budget ¥3,000–8,000 for a good Gyuto. Ask to sharpen before purchase.

items.notes: One of Tokyo's best hanami spots in early April.
items.address: Ueno Park, 5-20 Ueno Koen, Taito-ku, Tokyo 110-0007, Japan
items.detail:
Ueno Park (上野公園) — Hanami
Access: JR Yamanote Line to Ueno Station (Shinobazu Exit). ~20 min from Tsukiji.
Entry: Free

Ueno has 1,000+ cherry trees. In early April the blossoms canopy is extraordinary. Buy konbini picnic food and find a patch of grass — this is how locals do hanami.

While in Ueno:
- Tokyo National Museum: Japan's largest museum. ¥1,000pp. Extraordinary samurai armour, pottery, Buddhist sculpture. Allow 90 mins.
- Shinobazu Pond: The lotus pond at the south end. Paddle boats for hire.
- Ameya-Yokocho (Ameyoko): Busy street market directly outside Ueno Station — snacks, cheap cosmetics, dried fish.

items.notes: Ramen, izakaya or yakiniku. Options: Uobei (conveyor belt sushi), Han no Daidokoro (yakiniku).
items.address: Shibuya Crossing, 2-2-1 Dogenzaka, Shibuya-ku, Tokyo 150-0043, Japan
items.detail:
Dinner options around Shibuya for 4:

Uobei (魚べい): Touch-screen conveyor belt sushi — plates delivered by express rail to your seat. Very fun, ~¥1,500–2,000pp.

Fuunji (風雲児): Outstanding tsukemen (dipping ramen). Usually a 30 min queue outside. Near Shinjuku.

Gyukatsu Motomura: Lightly breaded beef cutlet you cook on a hot stone at your table. ¥1,500–2,000pp. Queue outside ~20 min, worth it.

Quick and local: Yoshinoya or Sukiya — Japanese beef bowl chains, 24hrs, ¥500–700pp. Not fancy but genuinely delicious and very Japanese.

days.title: Tokyo — Sumo · Tsukiji · Ueno

ideas.title: Sumo Museum (free)
ideas.notes: Free entry. In the Ryogoku Kokugikan sumo stadium. Good intro to sumo history and culture. Near the Oshiage stable area.

ideas.title: Chankonabe (sumo stew)
ideas.notes: Traditional sumo wrestler diet. Restaurants near Ryogoku station. Hearty, warming, very local. About ¥1,500–2,500pp.

ideas.title: TeamLab Planets (Toyosu)
ideas.notes: Immersive digital art. Barefoot through water. Otherworldly. ⚠ Book 2 months in advance — 9am slots are hardest to get.

ideas.title: Tokyo Skytree observation deck
ideas.notes: Tallest structure in Japan. Observation deck + Solamachi shopping at the base. Book midnight, 30 days in advance.

ideas.title: Shimokitazawa neighbourhood
ideas.notes: Very un-touristy. Strong coffee culture, unique thrift shops. Short train from Shinjuku. Very different vibe to central Tokyo.

ideas.title: Gotokuji Temple (lucky cats)
ideas.notes: Hundreds of ceramic maneki-neko (lucky cat) figures. Very photogenic. Near Shimokitazawa.

ideas.title: Standing sushi bar
ideas.notes: Must-do Tokyo experience. Look for ones near Tsukiji or around major train stations. Fast, cheap, excellent quality.

ideas.title: Ichiran Ramen
ideas.notes: Solo-booth tonkotsu ramen. 24 hours. You order on a sheet, food arrives through a hatch. No social pressure. Classic.

ideas.title: [JACK] Age.3 Ginza — fried sweet sandwiches
ideas.notes: Ginza 1-24-11 Sugiura Building 1F. Takeout-only specialty shop for deep-fried sandwiches — bread fried to order, then immediately filled. 30+ flavours, sweet and savoury. Must-order: Crème Brûlée (torched custard, very good), Chocolate Cream Strawberry, Ganache Matcha. Savoury options: Beef Yakiniku (excellent), Egg Salad. Around ¥850 each. Queue 30–60 min, especially weekends — go early morning or off-peak. Park across the street for eating. Takeout only, no seating.

ideas.title: [JACK] American Diner Andra — bechamel burger
ideas.notes: Higashi-Ueno, Taito-ku — close to Ueno and Asakusa. Signature dish: smash burger cut in half and lowered into a hot iron skillet of sizzling béchamel-style cheese sauce. Theatrical, flavourful, messy. American diner décor (sports jerseys, 80s videos, red/white/blue). 5 Chome-13-7 Higashiueno. Lunch 11am–2pm, Dinner 5pm–9pm. Closed Sundays. Very small (4 tables + 4 counter seats) — arrive before opening or expect a wait. Sign the name book outside first.

ideas.title: [JACK] Ginza Hachigo — Michelin ramen
ideas.notes: ⚠️ BOOK THIS NOW — see checklist. Only 6 seats. Michelin-recognised French-trained chef makes extraordinary clear-broth ramen: duck, chicken, prosciutto, shellfish — no soy tare. Light, velvety, complex. Signature: Ajitama Chuka Soba with soft egg + tender chashu. Special version adds foie gras and truffle dumpling (+¥300). Side chashu rice worth it. Reservations via TableCheck open Saturdays 9am Japan time for following week. Walk-up: queue from 9am, ~30 bowls per day, sells out fast. Ginza 3-14-2.

ideas.title: [JACK] Wagyu Mafia — katsu sando
ideas.notes: Nakameguro, 2 min from Nakameguro Station. Standing counter, 6 stools. Wagyu/Kobe beef chateaubriand deep-fried and served in milk bread — the katsu sando elevated to absurd levels. Multiple cuts available: Sirloin (¥5,000), Chateaubriand (¥18,000+), limited Kobe Champion (¥110,000 — 20 per year, sells in 10 mins). Jack probably isn't after the ¥110k one. The sirloin is the move. Open 11am–9pm. Closed Wednesdays. Nakameguro Atlas Tower 1F, 1-26-1 Kamimeguro.
ideas.mapQuery: Wagyu Mafia Cutlet Sandwich Nakameguro Tokyo

ideas.title: [JACK] The Front Room — milk bread pudding
ideas.notes: Marunouchi Building 1F, 2-4-1 Marunouchi, Chiyoda-ku — directly beside Tokyo Station. All-day café and bakery in an office tower. Jack specifically recommends the milk bread pudding. Great stop if passing through Tokyo Station (e.g. on the way back from a day out).
ideas.mapQuery: The Front Room Marunouchi Tokyo Station

ideas.title: [JACK] Benizuru — Japanese pancakes
ideas.notes: Nishi-Asakusa, Taito-ku. Pancake-focused brunch café. 2-1-11 Nishi-Asakusa. Walkable from Asakusa and Tawaramachi Stations — good add-on to Senso-ji visit. Jack recommends for the pancakes (Japanese-style thick hotcakes).

ideas.title: [JACK] Fukushimaya — oden, daikon radish
ideas.notes: Azabu-Juban, Minato-ku — 2 min from Azabu-Juban Station. Founded 1921. Over 100 years of oden. Ground floor is takeaway fish cakes (satsuma-age); second floor is a cosy izakaya. Signature: miso oden (Maruya Haccho Miso), daikon radish soaked black in broth, beef tendon. Jack mentions fried radish — likely the daikon tempura side dish. Lunch sets very good value. English menu available.

ideas.title: [JACK] Izumo — from Osaka (Okayama day trip)
ideas.notes: Jack mentions Izumo — this is almost certainly Izumo Taisha, the ancient Shinto grand shrine in Shimane Prefecture, one of Japan's most sacred sites. It is a significant detour from your route (4–5 hrs from Osaka by Shinkansen + limited express). Not realistic on the current itinerary. However, if Jack means Okayama Day Trip instead — Himeji is already Day 13. Confirm with Jack what exactly he means.

Hakone Tozan Railway: Hakone-Yumoto → Gora
Duration: ~40 minutes · Covered by Freepass
The train zigzags up the mountain using switchbacks — the driver gets out at each switchback to switch ends. Unusual and memorable.
The train climbs through dense cedar and maple forest. In early April some cherry blossoms may still be visible in the valley.
Alight at Gora Station (end of the line). Hotel shuttle pickup at Gora Station Bus Stop #1 — follow signs.

items.detail:
Kaiseki Buffet Dinner — TEN-YU
F&B credit: ¥5,605 per room (2 rooms = ¥11,210 total credit)
Dress: Wear your yukata — this is expected and part of the experience.

Kaiseki is Japan's multi-course haute cuisine — small, exquisite seasonal dishes.
Expect: Sashimi station, grilled fish and wagyu, mountain vegetables (sansai), chawanmushi (savoury egg custard), miso soup, rice, pickles, desserts.

After dinner: Arrange Takuhaibin at the front desk before going to bed. Then rotenburo.

items.notes: In-room outdoor hot spring bath. Best after dinner.
items.address: Hakone Kowakien Ten-yu, 1297 Ninotaira, Hakone, Ashigarashimo District, Kanagawa 250-0407, Japan
items.detail:
Private Rotenburo (露天風呂) — In-Room Outdoor Onsen

Your room has a private outdoor hot spring bath on the balcony. Water fed directly from Hakone's volcanic springs.

Onsen etiquette:
- Shower and wash thoroughly before entering the bath
- Enter slowly — water is very hot (42°C+)
- No soap in the bath itself
- Drink water before and after — the heat is dehydrating

Best time: After dinner, under the night sky. The mineral water (sodium bicarbonate) leaves skin noticeably soft.
Communal baths: TEN-YU also has indoor/outdoor shared baths separated by gender — worth trying for the different atmosphere.

items.detail:
Hakone Tozan Cable Car: Gora → Sounzan
Duration: ~10 minutes · 4 stops · Covered by Freepass
Gradient: The steepest cable car in Japan.
The car climbs steeply through dense forest. Views open up as you approach the summit.
At Sounzan: Transfer immediately to the Hakone Ropeway gondola — the two systems connect seamlessly.

items.notes: Aerial gondola over volcanic ridgeline. Mt. Fuji framed ahead on clear mornings. Covered by Freepass.
items.address: Sounzan Station, Gora, Hakone, Kanagawa 250-0408, Japan

items.notes: Active volcanic zone. Sulphur steam venting from cracked earth. Short hiking trail (~30 min loop). Food stalls for snacks. Allow 1 hour. Check trail status on arrival — occasionally closed due to volcanic activity.
items.address: Owakudani, Hakone, Ashigarashimo District, Kanagawa 250-0631, Japan
items.detail:
Owakudani (大涌谷) — Great Boiling Valley
Entry: Free to walk around the main viewing platform
Hiking trail: ¥1,000pp when open — 30 min loop through the volcanic zone

Active volcanic zone formed 3,000 years ago. Sulphur gases vent from cracked yellow earth. The smell of eggs (hydrogen sulphide) hangs in the air.

Kuro Tamago (black eggs): Boiled in the volcanic spring, turning shells black. Sold at stalls (¥600 for 5). Legend: each adds 7 years to your life. ⚠ Stalls may be closed — check on arrival.

Views: Mt Fuji is framed perfectly ahead on clear days. Often the single best Fuji view of the trip.

items.notes: Final descent to Lake Ashi. Covered by Freepass.
items.address: Owakudani, Hakone, Ashigarashimo District, Kanagawa 250-0631, Japan

items.activity: Lake Ashi — Hakone Pirate Ship Cruise
items.notes: Togendai → Moto-Hakone. ~40 min. Covered by Freepass. No reservation — allow up to 30 min wait for next departure. Disembark at MOTO-HAKONE (not Hakone-machi).
items.address: Togendai Pier, Hakone, Ashigarashimo District, Kanagawa 250-0522, Japan
items.detail:
Hakone Lake Ashi Sightseeing Cruise (Pirate Ship)
Route: Togendai → Moto-Hakone
Duration: ~40 minutes
Covered by Hakone Freepass — show pass at boarding gate
No reservation needed — board on arrival at Togendai terminal

Allow up to 30 minutes wait for the next departure.
Approximate departures from Togendai: 09:30 / 10:30 / 11:30 / 12:30 / 13:30 / 14:30 / 15:00

The "pirate ship" is a full-scale replica galleon with multiple decks, rigging and mock cannon ports.
Upper decks give the best views.

What to see from the boat:
- Hakone Shrine red torii gate rising from the lake
- Mt Fuji reflected in the lake on clear days
- Ropeway gondolas on the hillside
- Cedar forested mountains all around

Make sure to disembark at MOTO-HAKONE port — not Hakone-machi (one stop further).
Moto-Hakone is closer to the shrine, Shinsho Soba and the cedar avenue.

items.notes: 5 min walk to Shinsho Soba. 10 min walk to Hakone Shrine.
1 min walk from Moto-Hakone port. No reservation needed. Local favourite soba restaurant. Cash preferred. Allow 30-40 mins.
items.detail:
Shinsho Soba
Location: 1 minute walk from Moto-Hakone port, near the Hakone Shrine approach
No reservation needed · Cash preferred

Local soba restaurant right by Moto-Hakone port. Popular with locals. Fast, good value, no fuss.

What to order:
- Zaru soba (cold buckwheat noodles with dipping sauce) — the classic in spring
- Kake soba (hot buckwheat noodles in dashi broth) — warming after the ropeway
- Tempura soba — more substantial option

Soba is one of Hakone's local specialities — mountain spring water makes excellent noodles.
Allow 30-40 minutes for a relaxed lunch before the shrine visit.

After lunch, Hakone Shrine is a 10 min walk along the lakeshore.

items.notes: Ancient cedar path to the famous red torii gate rising from the lake. Free to enter.
items.address: Hakone Shrine, 80-1 Motohakone, Hakone, Ashigarashimo-gun, Kanagawa 250-0522, Japan
items.detail:
Hakone Shrine (箱根神社)
Address: 80-1 Motohakone · Entry: Free · Open all hours
Access: 10 min walk from Shinsho Soba along the lakeshore

Founded 757 AD. Approach is through a tunnel of ancient cedars.

The Peace Torii: The iconic red torii gate standing in Lake Ashi — reached via a path from the main shrine down to the water's edge. Best at dawn or dusk.

Main shrine: Good for omamori (protective charms) — Hakone Shrine charms for safe travel are sold here.
Amazake-chaya teahouse is 15 min walk from the shrine along the Old Tokaido Highway.

items.notes: 350-year-old thatched teahouse on the old Tokaido Highway. Warm amazake and mochi ~¥500pp.
items.address: Amazake-chaya, 395-1 Moto-Hakone, Hakone, Kanagawa 250-0522, Japan
items.detail:
Amazake-chaya (甘酒茶屋)
Address: 395-1 Moto-Hakone · Hours: 07:00–17:30 · Price: ~¥500pp

A beautifully preserved 350-year-old thatched-roof teahouse on the original Edo-era Tokaido Highway. Serving travellers continuously for 300+ years.

Amazake: Warm, sweet, fermented rice drink. Very low/no alcohol. Earthy, warming, slightly sweet. Traditional post-journey restorative.
Mochi: Freshly pounded rice cakes with kinako (roasted soybean flour).

The setting: Dark wooden interior, open fire, smell of wood smoke, cedar trees outside.

items.notes: 1km Edo-era road flanked by 400-year-old cedar trees. Free. Connects Moto-Hakone to Hakone-machi. Allow 30 mins.
items.address: Hakone Shrine, 80-1 Motohakone, Hakone, Ashigarashimo-gun, Kanagawa 250-0522, Japan
items.detail:
Old Tokaido Cedar Avenue (旧東海道杉並木)
Length: ~1km · Free · Flat, easy walk
Route: From Amazake-chaya south to Hakone-machi

The original feudal highway between Edo (Tokyo) and Kyoto. The cedar trees are 400 years old, planted in the early Edo period as shade for travellers. Towering, dramatic, beautifully atmospheric.

The avenue ends at Hakone-machi — a small lakeside village with souvenir shops and the Old Hakone Checkpoint museum (Edo-period checkpoint, ¥500pp if interested).

items.notes: From Hakone-machi bus stop (end of Cedar Avenue). Covered by Freepass. ~30-40 min journey back toward TEN-YU area.

items.notes: 5 min walk from hotel. Covered 400-year-old market, 5 blocks, 100+ stalls. Kyoto pickles, yuba tofu, matcha sweets, grilled skewers. Close at 18:00 — go now while it's still open. Perfect first taste of Kyoto.
items.detail:
Nishiki Market (錦市場) — Kyoto's Kitchen
Distance from hotel: 5 min walk
Hours: 09:00–18:00 (most stalls). Go before 18:00.
Entry: Free to walk through

A covered arcade market running 5 blocks through central Kyoto. Over 100 stalls, many run by the same families for generations. Known as "Kyoto's Kitchen" — the best place to taste what makes Kyoto food distinctive.

What to try:
- Kyoto tsukemono (pickles) — dozens of varieties, beautiful colours
- Yuba (silky tofu skin) — Kyoto speciality, served on skewers
- Tamagoyaki (sweet rolled omelette) on a stick
- Matcha sweets and mochi
- Grilled wagyu skewers
- Fresh seasonal fruit

In April look for: sakura-flavoured wagashi, spring takenoko (bamboo shoots), cherry blossom pickles.

Tip: It's narrow and gets busy — go with the flow, eat as you walk (stalls provide napkins). Cash and IC cards accepted at most stalls.

At the eastern end: Nishiki Tenmangu Shrine — tiny, historic, with a magnificent old camphor tree. Worth a quick look.

items.activity: Kamo River walk — Sanjo to Shijo
items.notes: 5 min walk from Nishiki. Cherry blossoms along both banks. Flat, easy, beautiful. Locals picnicking on the riverbanks. The classic Kyoto evening stroll.
items.detail:
Kamo River (鴨川) — Sanjo to Shijo stretch
Distance from Nishiki: 5 min walk east
Entry: Free · Open 24hrs · Flat, easy walking

The Kamo River is the beating heart of Kyoto. The riverbanks between Sanjo and Shijo bridges are one of the city's great gathering places — locals sit on the stone steps watching the water, cyclists pass, herons stand motionless in the shallows.

In early April: Cherry trees line both banks in full bloom. The reflection of blossoms in the river in the late afternoon light is one of Kyoto's most beautiful sights. Locals spread picnic mats on the grass for hanami (blossom viewing).

The Shirakawa Canal, a smaller branch that runs parallel through southern Gion, is even more intimate — cherry trees hang directly over the water between traditional machiya townhouses. Branch off from the main river and walk along it toward Gion if you want a quieter, more atmospheric route.

Walk north from Shijo Bridge to Sanjo Bridge (~15 min) or south — either direction is lovely. The riverside steps make a perfect spot to sit with a coffee from a nearby konbini and just watch the city.

This walk connects naturally into Gion for an early evening wander.

items.notes: Kyoto's famous geisha district. 10 min walk from the river. Stone-paved alleys, wooden machiya teahouses, lanterns beginning to glow. Chance of spotting a maiko in the early evening. Free to wander.
items.detail:
Gion District — Hanamikoji & Shirakawa Canal
Distance from Kamo River: 10 min walk
Entry: Free to wander · Best 17:00–19:00

Gion is Kyoto's most famous geisha district and genuinely one of the most beautiful urban environments in Japan. The main street (Hanamikoji-dori) is lined with centuries-old wooden ochaya (teahouses) behind latticed facades.

Early evening is the best time — the lanterns start to glow as dusk falls, the tour groups thin out, and maiko (apprentice geisha) begin moving between appointments. Photography of maiko on the street is now discouraged out of respect — enjoy the encounter naturally.

Shirakawa Canal: The small canal running through southern Gion has cherry trees positioned along the water between traditional wooden machiya houses — one of Kyoto's most intimate blossom spots. Follow it east from the main Gion crossing.

Yasaka Shrine: At the top of Hanamikoji Street. Free, always open. Red lanterns, stone torii, the broad shrine grounds giving way to Maruyama Park beyond. Worth walking through toward the park if energy allows.

This area is also where Ponto-cho begins — the narrow alley running parallel to the river, perfect for dinner later.

items.notes: Optional — two covered shopping arcades 2 min from hotel. Teramachi is more traditional (antiques, stationery, knives). Shinkyogoku is younger and livelier (street food, fashion, souvenirs, arcades). Good if anyone wants to split off shopping.
items.detail:
Teramachi-dori & Shinkyogoku
Distance from hotel: 2 min walk
Hours: Most shops open until 20:00–21:00

Two parallel covered shopping arcades running north-south through central Kyoto, a 2-minute walk from the hotel.

Teramachi-dori (寺町通): The more atmospheric of the two. Traditional Kyoto shops — antiques, Japanese stationery and washi paper, handmade knives, tea shops, Buddhist goods. Some of the oldest continuous businesses in Kyoto are here. Worth a slow wander.

Shinkyogoku (新京極): Livelier, more youth-oriented. Street food, fashion shops, souvenir stalls, game arcades, cinemas. Jack and Charlie will find more here. Good for cheap Kyoto souvenirs.

The two arcades run parallel and are connected by several cross streets — easy to hop between them. Both lead south to Shijo-dori (the main shopping street) and north toward Nijo-dori.

Good option if the family wants to split — some wander Teramachi for traditional finds while others browse Shinkyogoku.

items.notes: ⚠ Book ahead — first night in Kyoto, cherry blossom season. Ponto-cho for kawayuka riverside dining, or Gion for a traditional Kyoto meal. Both a short walk from the hotel.
items.address: Pontocho, Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto 604-8094, Japan
items.detail:
First dinner in Kyoto — the start of what many consider the finest food city in Japan.

Ponto-cho (先斗町): The narrow alley running parallel to the Kamo River. 5 min walk from hotel. In April the kawayuka (riverside platforms) may just be opening. Best: Pontocho Fujita (kaiseki, Tablecheck), Pontocho Robin (hotpot, private rooms).

Gion (祇園): 10 min walk. More upscale. Tempura Endo is excellent (book ahead).

⚠ Book in advance — cherry blossom season is the busiest time in Kyoto.

Route: Walk 1 min to Karasuma-Shijo Station → Karasuma Line northbound → Kitaoji Station (3 stops, ¥260pp, ~9 min)
From Kitaoji: Bus 101 or 102 or taxi (~¥1,000) to Kinkaku-ji, 10–15 min.

Timing: Arrive at Kinkaku-ji by 09:30 to beat tour buses which start arriving from 10:00. The difference is significant.

items.notes: Gold-leaf pavilion reflecting in Mirror Pond. ¥1,200pp. Arrive early before crowds.
items.address: Kinkaku-ji Temple, 1 Kinkakujicho, Kita Ward, Kyoto 603-8361, Japan
items.detail:
Kinkaku-ji (金閣寺) — Temple of the Golden Pavilion
Hours: 09:00–17:00 · Entry: ¥1,200pp · Access: 15 min bus from Kitaoji

Three-storey pavilion covered in gold leaf reflecting in the Mirror Pond. UNESCO World Heritage.

Floors: Ground = Heian palace style (unpainted). Second = samurai style (gold). Third = Zen Buddhist style (entirely gold). Phoenix on roof = eternal life.

One-way path: Fixed clockwise route. Best photo spots at main viewing platform and Anmintaku Pond.
Souvenirs: Exit path has excellent matcha ice cream and Kinkaku-ji merchandise unique to the temple.

items.notes: Famous karesansui dry rock garden — 15 stones, no two ever visible at once. ¥800pp.
items.address: Ryoan-ji Temple, 13 Ryoanjigoranocho, Ukyo-ku, Kyoto 616-8001, Japan
items.detail:
Ryoan-ji (龍安寺) — Temple of the Peaceful Dragon
Hours: 08:00–17:00 · Entry: ¥800pp · Access: 15 min walk from Kinkaku-ji

The rock garden: 15 stones in groups of 7, 5 and 3, set in raked white gravel. 30m × 10m. From any angle within the veranda, one stone is always hidden — only all 15 visible from directly above.

The meaning: Deliberately enigmatic — no official interpretation given by the temple. Contemplation is the point.

Beyond the garden: The large Mirror Pond and woodland garden are beautiful and much less visited. Allow 30 mins to walk around.
Tip: Sit quietly in the veranda for 10 minutes. The experience is very different from hurrying through.

items.notes: Bus (Kyoto bus day pass ¥700/person) or taxi ~¥1,500. Allow 45–50 min on Saturday.
items.address: Ryoan-ji Temple, 13 Ryoanjigoranocho, Ukyo-ku, Kyoto 616-8001, Japan
items.detail:
Ginkaku-ji (銀閣寺) — Temple of the Silver Pavilion
Hours: 08:30–17:00 · Entry: ¥1,100pp

Despite the name, the pavilion was never actually covered in silver — the shogun ran out of money. The garden is the real draw:
- Kogetsudai: 1.8m high cone of raked sand representing Mt Fuji
- Sea of Silver Sand: raked gravel representing moonlit water
- Moss garden behind the main buildings — lush, perfectly maintained

From here: Philosopher's Path starts right outside the gates — walk south to Nanzen-ji (2km, ~45 min).

items.notes: Sea of Silver Sand moss garden. ¥1,100pp. Quieter than Kinkaku-ji.
items.address: Ginkaku-ji Temple, 2 Ginkakujicho, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto 606-8402, Japan

items.notes: 2km cherry tree-lined canal path from Ginkaku-ji towards Nanzen-ji. Most beautiful walk in cherry blossom season.
items.address: Ginkaku-ji Temple, 2 Ginkakujicho, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto 606-8402, Japan
items.detail:
Philosopher's Path (哲学の道)
Route: Ginkaku-ji → Nanzen-ji (~2km, ~45 min flat walk along a small canal) · Entry: Free

Named after philosopher Nishida Kitaro who meditated here daily on walks to Kyoto University.

In early April: Cherry trees arch over the canal, petals falling into the water. One of Kyoto's most celebrated hanami spots.

Along the path: Small cafés, art galleries, craft shops. Eikan-do Zenrin-ji is beautiful in spring — ¥1,000pp.

End of path: Nanzen-ji Aqueduct — a striking brick Roman-style aqueduct from the Meiji era running through the temple grounds. Very unusual. Free to walk under.

items.notes: Yudofu (tofu hotpot) or Omen noodle restaurant on the Path.
items.address: Nanzen-ji Temple, 86 Fukuchicho, Sakyo-ku, Kyoto 606-8435, Japan
items.detail:
Lunch options around Nanzen-ji:

Yudofu (湯豆腐): Kyoto's most traditional dish — silken tofu simmered in kombu dashi broth. Multiple dedicated restaurants near Nanzen-ji (Junsei, Okutan). ~¥2,500–3,500pp. Famous and busy — expect a short wait.

Omen (おめん): Famous handmade udon on the Philosopher's Path (north end). Thick, chewy noodles in a light broth. Queue moves fast. ~¥1,200–1,500pp. Very popular with locals.

items.activity: Return to hotel — rest before Gion
items.notes: Subway from Keage or Higashiyama Station back to Shijo/Kawaramachi.
items.address: Mitsui Garden Hotel Kyoto Kawaramachi Jokyoji, 563 Shimogoriomachi, Shimogyo Ward, Kyoto 600-8096, Japan
items.mapQuery: Mitsui Garden Hotel Kyoto Kawaramachi Jokyoji
items.detail:
Return to Mitsui Garden Hotel:
- From Nanzen-ji: Walk 10 min to Keage Station (Tozai Line) → west to Karasuma → change to Karasuma Line south to Shijo. ~35 mins.
- Or taxi ~¥1,200, 15 mins.

Rest and refresh before the evening — Gion dinner and night walk ahead.

items.notes: ⚠ BOOK NOW — Saturday in Gion during cherry blossom season fills weeks ahead. Tempura Endo or walk Hanamikoji.
items.address: Gion District, Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto 605-0073, Japan

items.notes: Beautifully lit machiya townhouses. Walk to Yasaka Shrine — free, open all night, stunning lanterns.
items.address: Gion District, Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto 605-0073, Japan
items.detail:
Gion after dark — arguably the most atmospheric urban experience of the trip.

Hanamikoji-dori at night: Ochaya (teahouses) glow with orange lantern light. Quieter than day but still lively. Between 20:00–22:00 you may see maiko being escorted to appointments. Quiet, respectful observation only.

Yasaka Shrine (八坂神社): Free, open all night. Stone lanterns lit, broad promenade beautiful. The Okera Lantern burns with sacred fire — used traditionally to light New Year fires in Kyoto homes.

Maruyama Park: Beyond the shrine. Giant illuminated weeping cherry (shidarezakura) in April. The park fills with picnickers and revellers. Very festive, very local.

days.title: Kyoto — Northern Temples · Gion

ideas.title: Otagi Nenbutsuji Temple
ideas.notes: 1,200 unique stone Rakan statues each with a different expression. Quiet, very photogenic. At the top of Arashiyama walking route.

ideas.title: Ōkōchi Sansō Villa
ideas.notes: 15 min uphill from Bamboo Grove. ¥1,000pp includes matcha tea. Mountain views. Less crowded than main Arashiyama sights.

ideas.title: Sannenzaka + Ninenzaka preserved streets
ideas.notes: Stone-paved streets below Kiyomizudera. Traditional machiya townhouses, Yasaka Pagoda views. Very photogenic.

ideas.title: Kiyomizudera spring illumination
ideas.notes: Special night illumination during cherry blossom season in April. Book ahead — separate ticket from daytime entry.

ideas.title: Yasaka Shrine at night
ideas.notes: Open all night. Stunning paper lanterns along Hanamikoji. Free. Best after Gion dinner.

ideas.title: Tempura Endo, Gion
ideas.notes: Excellent counter tempura in Gion. Book ahead — very popular on Saturday evenings.

items.notes: Free breakfast included. Aim to leave hotel by 07:30 to beat the crowds at the bamboo grove.
items.mapQuery: Mitsui Garden Hotel Kyoto Kawaramachi Jokyoji
items.detail:
Early start today. Breakfast at 07:00, leave by 07:30.
The Arashiyama Bamboo Grove is genuinely magical before 08:30 and genuinely awful after 10:00 in cherry blossom season. The early start makes a significant difference.

items.notes: From hotel: walk to Kawaramachi Station, Hankyu to Kyoto, then JR Sagano Line to JR Saga-Arashiyama (17 min, ¥240pp). Torokko Saga Station is adjacent to JR Saga-Arashiyama Station south exit — 1 min walk.
items.detail:
Route: Hotel → Hankyu Kawaramachi (1 min walk) → Hankyu to Kyoto-Kawaramachi → JR at Kyoto Station → JR Sagano Line to JR Saga-Arashiyama
Total: ~30 mins · Cost: ~¥600pp

Torokko Saga Station: Adjacent to JR Saga-Arashiyama south exit — 1 min walk. Sagano Romantic Train ticket gate opens ~08:35.
Tip: Bamboo Grove is right next to the station — go there first before collecting train tickets (grove is open from dawn).

items.notes: Go now — this is one of the most crowded spots in Japan during cherry blossom season. 08:00 is genuinely quiet. Nonomiya Shrine is hidden inside the grove — small black torii, very atmospheric. Free.
items.detail:
Arashiyama Bamboo Grove (嵐山竹林の小径)
Entry: Free · Open from dawn
Path: 400m long. Stems 20m high, swaying gently, extraordinary filtered light. In early morning (before 09:00) genuinely one of the most beautiful places in Japan. After 10:00 a tourist crush.

Nonomiya Shrine: Hidden inside the grove. Small black-lacquered torii gate on the right. Dedicated to matchmaking and safe childbirth. Very quiet even when the grove is busy.

Wander the lanes branching off the main path — equally beautiful and much quieter.

items.notes: UNESCO World Heritage garden. Cherry blossoms over the pond. ¥500pp for garden, ¥300 extra for main hall. Allow 45 mins.
items.detail:
Tenryu-ji (天龍寺)
Hours: 08:30–17:30 · Entry: ¥500pp (garden) · ¥800pp (garden + main hall)

Founded 1339 by shogun Ashikaga Takauji. Garden by Muso Soseki — Japan's greatest garden designer. UNESCO World Heritage.

The main garden (Sogenchi): Large pond garden framed by Arashiyama mountain — 'borrowed scenery' (shakkei) technique where the surrounding mountain becomes part of the composition. In early April cherry trees around the pond are spectacular.

Main hall: The painted cloud and dragon ceiling (by Kayama Matazo, 1997) is worth the extra ¥300.
From Tenryu-ji: Walk out through the rear gate directly into the bamboo grove or toward Torokko Saga Station.

items.notes: 1 min from JR Saga-Arashiyama Station south exit. Collect e-ticket at gate via the mobile voucher link — do NOT use the printed PDF.
items.detail:
Walk to Torokko Saga Station — 1 min from JR Saga-Arashiyama Station south exit.

⚠ Ticket collection: Use the e-ticket link linkt.to/oGdvHgf1 — show this screen at the ticket gate. Do NOT use the printed PDF voucher.

Arrive at the gate by 09:55 to allow time to check in and find your carriage (Car 3) before the 10:02 departure.

Boarding: Torokko Saga Station is 1 min from JR Saga-Arashiyama Station south exit. Business hours open ~08:35. Timetable: sagano-kanko.co.jp/en/train-info

Seat changes allowed up to 1 day before departure (max 3 changes).

items.activity: Arrive Torokko Kameoka — shuttle to boat dock
items.notes: Free shuttle bus from Torokko Kameoka Station to Hozugawa boat dock (~15 min). Alternatively a horse-drawn carriage connects the two. Departures timed to the train arrivals.
items.mapQuery: Hozugawa River Boat Ride Kameoka Kyoto
items.detail:
At Torokko Kameoka Station:
Free shuttle bus to Hozugawa boat dock: Timed to meet incoming trains. Walk out of the station and follow staff directions to the bus. Journey ~15 mins.
Horse-drawn carriage: Also available — slower, more expensive, but memorable.

At the dock: Check in for your 11:00 Hozugawa River Cruise. Show Klook QR code at the reception desk. Life jackets issued here.
⚠ No restrooms on the boat — use facilities before boarding.

items.notes: ✅ BOOKED via Klook · £99.93 · 4 adults (Andrew, Sarah, Jack, Charlie Evans) · Booking: KLOOK-20260323-DB5V · Participation time: 11:00 · Allow up to 30 min wait at dock before departure · Arrives Arashiyama ~13:15 · Show smartphone QR code ticket at reception desk on arrival
items.mapQuery: Hozugawa River Boat Ride Kameoka Kyoto
items.detail:
Booking ref: KLOOK-20260323-DB5V · Provider: 保津川下り（保津川遊船企業組合）
Travellers: Andrew Evans, Sarah Evans, Jack Evans, Charlie Evans · 4 adults
Date: Sun 12 Apr 2026 · Participation time: 11:00 · £99.93 paid via Klook · Purchased: 23 Mar 2026

TICKET: Show smartphone QR code at the reception desk at the boarding area.
Location: 2 Shimo-Nakajima Hozu-cho Kameoka, 621-0005, Kyoto
Google Maps: https://goo.gl/maps/8pULEHy6xMJswXDj8

DEPARTURE: Regular departures at 9:00 / 10:00 / 11:00 / 12:00 / 13:00 / 14:00 / 15:00
Your slot: 11:00 — allow up to 30 minutes after scheduled time before the boat departs.
If you arrive late for your check-in time, boarding may not be permitted.

WHAT'S INCLUDED: 2-hour boat ride along the Hozugawa River with skilled boatmen.
Life jackets compulsory — provided at the boarding point.
No restrooms on the boat — use facilities before boarding.
Food and beverages are permitted on board.
Photography allowed — inform boatman if you intend to stand up.

WEATHER: Runs in light rain (covered by canopy). May be suspended in severe weather or river flooding.
Check operation status on the day: hozugawakudari.jp

ACCESS FROM TOROKKO KAMEOKA: Free shuttle bus from Torokko Kameoka Station to the boat dock (~15 min). Horse-drawn carriage also available. Departures timed to the train arrivals.
Alternatively: 10 min walk from JR Kameoka Station (North exit).

items.notes: Boat docks just below Togetsukyo Bridge. 5 min walk to Arashiyama Nishiki restaurant on Nakanoshibashi island.
items.detail:
The boat docks at the base of Togetsukyo Bridge (渡月橋) in central Arashiyama.
Togetsukyo Bridge itself is worth pausing on — views of the river and wooded mountains. In April cherry blossoms frame the mountain backdrop.
Arashiyama Nishiki restaurant is on Nakanoshibashi island (the small island in the river) — 5 min walk from the dock.

items.notes: ⚠ BOOK AHEAD — Sunday cherry blossom season. Traditional kaiseki ryotei on Nakanoshibashi island beside Togetsukyo Bridge. Sakura-juku-zen seasonal box-set in April. ☎ 075-871-8888 · Hours 11:00–21:00 L.O. 19:00 · Closed Tuesdays · 4 min from Hankyu Arashiyama Station
15 min uphill walk from the bridge. Villa of 1920s samurai film star — stunning gardens over the Kyoto valley. ¥1,000pp includes matcha and tea. Worth it if energy allows after the cruise.
items.detail:
Ōkōchi Sansō Villa (大河内山荘)
Entry: ¥1,000pp (includes matcha and wagashi sweet)
Access: 15 min uphill walk from Togetsukyo Bridge · Hours: 09:00–17:00

Villa and gardens of Ōkōchi Denjiro, a famous 1920s samurai film actor. Spread across the hillside above Arashiyama with panoramic views over the entire Kyoto valley.

Multiple garden styles — Japanese stroll garden, raked gravel, moss gardens, tea garden. The matcha rest stop at the end of the garden route is a welcome pause.

Best views: From the upper terrace looking south over the city. On clear days visible all the way to Osaka.

items.notes: Riverside wander, shops, Tenryu-ji garden exit, Togetsukyo Bridge photos with the mountains.
items.detail:
Last free time in Arashiyama:
- Riverside stroll along the Oi River south of the bridge
- Arashiyama shops: Good craft shops, matcha ice cream, bamboo goods
- Sagano bamboo crafts — the area has excellent bamboo products
- Café break: Several good independent cafés near the Hankyu station

items.notes: Hankyu Arashiyama Line from Arashiyama Station → Katsura → Kawaramachi (35 min, ¥270pp). Or JR from Saga-Arashiyama → Kyoto Station (17 min, ¥240pp).
items.detail:
Two return options:
1. Hankyu Arashiyama Line: Hankyu Arashiyama Station → Katsura → Kawaramachi. ~35 min, ¥270pp. Drops very close to the hotel. Recommended.
2. JR Sagano Line: JR Saga-Arashiyama → Kyoto Station. ~17 min, ¥240pp. Faster but requires subway or taxi from Kyoto Station.

items.notes: ⚠ BOOK AHEAD — Sunday evening cherry blossom season, this will be very busy. Riverside kawayuka terrace dining. Book as soon as possible.
items.detail:
Ponto-cho Sunday dinner — book in advance.

Recommended:
- Pontocho Robin (Rubin): Traditional Japanese hotpot (duck, kinmedai). Private rooms 4–6 people. River views. ¥8,800–16,500pp. Tabelog bookable.
- Pontocho Fujita: Kaiseki, dashi-focused, private rooms. Tablecheck bookable.
- Via hotel concierge: Ask Mitsui Garden to call on your behalf — most reliable for cherry blossom season.

Kawayuka: Riverside dining platforms may just be opening in mid-April — worth asking when you call.

days.title: Kyoto — Arashiyama

ideas.notes: 50 min boat ride down the Hozu River gorge from Kameoka to Arashiyama. Scenic and peaceful. ⚠ Book in advance.

items.activity: ⭐ Fushimi Inari — thousands of torii gates
items.notes: One of Japan's most iconic sights. 10,000+ vermillion torii gates winding 4km up Mt Inari. Monday morning is much quieter than weekends. Walk to first viewing platform (~45 min return). Free, open 24hrs.

items.detail:
Dotonbori (道頓堀) — Osaka's most famous district
Access: Osaka Metro Midosuji Line from Umeda → Namba (3 stops, ¥230pp) or ~25 min walk south

Dotonbori is the neon heart of Osaka. The canal-side strip has the densest concentration of restaurants in Japan.

- Glico Running Man: LED billboard above the canal. Cross Ebisu-bashi bridge and look back — that's the classic shot.
- Kani Doraku: The moving mechanical crab sign (a restaurant). Another Osaka icon.
- Street food: Takoyaki (octopus balls) — dozens of competing stalls. Also kushikatsu and okonomiyaki.

Vibe: Louder, grittier and more fun than Kyoto. Japan's most extroverted city.

items.notes: Osaka's answer to Harajuku. Vintage stores, streetwear, record shops.
items.address: Amerikamura, Chuo Ward, Osaka 542-0086, Japan
items.detail:
Amerikamura (アメリカ村) — American Village
Access: 5 min walk west of Dotonbori · Shinsaibashi-suji arcade connects the two

Osaka's equivalent of Harajuku/Shimokitazawa. Radiates from Triangle Park.

What's here:
- Vintage and thrift: 2nd Street, Super Bookoff. Jack's territory.
- Streetwear: Supreme, Carhartt, local Japanese streetwear labels
- Record shops: Good vinyl selection
- Food: American-style diners, unique cafés

days.title: Kyoto → Osaka

ideas.title: Heian Jingu Shrine
ideas.notes: Enormous 24m vermillion torii — one of Japan's largest. Free to approach. Garden ¥1,500pp optional. 5 min from Okazaki area.

ideas.title: Tea ceremony (final Kyoto morning)
ideas.notes: Multiple operators. Camellia Tea Experience recommended — English-speaking, 75 min, near Higashiyama. Book ahead.

ideas.title: Nishiki Market — last Kyoto browse
ideas.notes: Kyoto's kitchen. Last chance for pickles, yuba tofu, matcha sweets. Stalls close ~18:00. 5 min walk from hotel.

ideas.title: Dotonbori at night (Osaka arrival eve)
ideas.notes: Much better after dark with all the neon. Glico Running Man, Kani Doraku crab sign. Walk the canal. Don't plan too much — just wander.

ideas.title: [JACK] Namba Parks — Osaka
ideas.notes: 9-floor mall built on the former Osaka baseball stadium footprint. Directly connected to Nankai Namba Station. 120+ shops and 40+ restaurants. Famous for the tiered terraced rooftop garden (Parks Garden) with 400+ plant species — free to walk. Fashion floors 1–5, restaurants floors 6–8. Good views of Osaka from upper levels. Opening hours: shops 11am–9pm, restaurants 11am–11pm.

items.notes: Kintetsu-Nara Station is 5 min walk from Nara Park. Deer will find you almost immediately.
items.address: Kintetsu Nara Station, Nara 630-8122, Japan
items.detail:
From Kintetsu-Nara Station: walk east ~5 min and you're in Nara Park. The deer roam freely on the path between the station and Todai-ji — you'll encounter them before you even reach the park proper.

Deer crackers (shika-senbei): Buy from vendors for ¥200 a bundle. The deer bow to ask for them — a trained behaviour. Watch your bags.

items.notes: 1,200+ free-roaming deer. Sacred in Shinto — believed to be messengers of the gods. Deer crackers ¥200 from vendors.
items.address: Nara Park, 469 Zoshicho, Nara 630-8212, Japan
items.detail:
Nara Park (奈良公園)
The park is enormous — 660 hectares. The deer are everywhere.

The deer are designated national treasures. They've lived here for 1,300 years. They're accustomed to people but are still wild — watch young children and don't tease them.

Best photo spots: Deer in front of the Nandaimon gate (huge wooden entrance to Todai-ji), deer on the broad grass lawns south of Todai-ji, deer near the stone lanterns at Kasuga Taisha approach.

items.notes: World's largest wooden building. Daibutsu (Great Buddha) is 15m tall — extraordinary scale. ¥1,000pp. Allow 45 min.
items.address: Todai-ji Temple, 406-1 Zoshicho, Nara 630-8211, Japan
items.detail:
Todai-ji (東大寺) — Eastern Great Temple
Hours: 07:30–17:30 · Entry: ¥1,000pp

The Daibutsu (Great Buddha): 15m tall bronze Buddha, cast in 743 AD. The scale is genuinely overwhelming — you turn a corner inside the hall and it fills your vision. One of Japan's defining experiences.

The hall itself: Rebuilt in 1709 — still the world's largest wooden structure. Even after being rebuilt at 2/3 the original size.

Nandaimon Gate: The giant wooden gate before the hall with two fearsome nio guardian statues — worth stopping to look properly.

items.notes: Kakinoha-zushi (persimmon leaf sushi) is Nara's signature dish. Several restaurants near Todai-ji. Budget ¥1,500–2,500pp.
items.address: Nara Park, 469 Zoshicho, Nara 630-8212, Japan
items.detail:
Nara food specialities:
- Kakinoha-zushi: Pressed sushi wrapped in persimmon leaves — Nara's most distinctive local dish. The leaves add a subtle flavour and were originally used as a preservative. Look for Hiraso or Tanaka-ya near the park.
- Miwa somen: Fine white noodles, served cold or in hot broth. Nara is the origin of somen noodles in Japan.
- Kakigori: Shaved ice with matcha syrup — excellent in the Higashimuki arcade area.

items.notes: UNESCO World Heritage. 3,000 stone and bronze lanterns — lit twice a year. Free to approach; inner shrine ¥500pp. 10 min walk east of Todai-ji.
items.address: Kasuga Taisha Shrine, 160 Kasuganocho, Nara 630-8212, Japan
items.detail:
Kasuga Taisha (春日大社) — UNESCO World Heritage
Hours: 06:30–17:30 · Outer shrine: Free · Inner sanctuary: ¥500pp

Founded in 768 AD. The approach through the forest is lined with over 3,000 stone lanterns, all donated by worshippers. The deer wander freely here too.

The hanging bronze lanterns inside the covered corridor are extraordinary — imagine all 2,000 lit at once (only happens during Mandoro festivals in February and August).

items.notes: Regular Kintetsu Express: Kintetsu-Nara → Osaka-Namba · ~35 min · ¥680pp. No reservation. Buy tickets at vending machine.
items.address: Kintetsu Nara Station, Nara 630-8122, Japan
items.detail:
Regular Kintetsu Express (no reservation needed).
Frequency: Every 15–30 min throughout the day.
Cost: ¥680pp × 4 = ¥2,720 total. Buy at vending machine at Kintetsu-Nara Station.
Journey: ~35 min direct to Osaka-Namba.

items.notes: Back at hotel by ~17:30. Free time before dinner.
items.address: Candeo Hotels Osaka The Tower, 5-2-28 Shibata, Kita-ku, Osaka 530-0012, Japan

items.notes: ⚠ STILL NEEDS BOOKING. Proper yakiniku — BBQ wagyu at the table. ~¥4,000–5,000pp. Namba area.
items.address: Namba, Chuo Ward, Osaka 542-0075, Japan
items.detail:
Osaka yakiniku dinner — book in advance.

Osaka has an extraordinary yakiniku culture — the largest concentration of Korean-Japanese communities in Japan developed this cuisine here.

What to order:
- Karubi (short rib): The classic cut, fatty and full of flavour
- Harami (outside skirt): Tender, well-marbled, quick to cook
- Tongue (tan): Thin sliced, grilled quickly, dipped in lemon
- Wagyu special course: Worth the splurge for one proper wagyu night

Budget: ¥4,000–5,000pp for a good set course with drinks.

ideas.notes: ¥1,400pp for 8F observation deck. Park free — excellent cherry blossoms in mid-April. ~10 min from Higashi-Umeda on Tanimachi Line.

ideas.notes: Twin towers with floating observatory ring. ¥1,500pp. Great at sunset. Only 15 min walk from Candeo hotel.

ideas.title: Hozenji Yokocho alley
ideas.notes: Tiny atmospheric alley near Dotonbori. Moss-covered Fudo statue, candlelit restaurants. Perfect slow pre-dinner wander.

ideas.title: Den Den Town (Osaka Akihabara)
ideas.notes: Osaka's electronics and anime district. Retro game shops, Gunpla (Gundam models), anime figures. Jack and Charlie in their element.

ideas.title: Kushikatsu — DO NOT double dip
ideas.notes: Shinsekai is the home of kushikatsu. Deep-fried breadcrumbed skewers. ⚠ Cardinal sin to double-dip the communal sauce. ~¥200 per skewer.

ideas.title: Kobe beef dinner (splurge night)
ideas.notes: One high-end wagyu meal. Worth it at least once. Look for certified Kobe beef restaurant. ¥8,000–15,000pp. Book ahead.

ideas.title: Melon pan ice cream, Dotonbori
ideas.notes: Freshly baked melon bread with soft-serve inside. Very photogenic. Very Osaka.

ideas.title: [JACK] Acorn Vintage — Osaka
ideas.notes: Described by experts as simply the most curated high-end vintage shop in Japan. Very small and selective — around 200 pieces at a time, only the finest examples: sun-faded M65 jackets in three perfect sizes, big-E Levi's in wearable condition, French moleskin workwear, faded hoodies. Not cheap, but genuinely exceptional. Worth hunting down the address (search Tabelog or Instagram @acorn_osaka before visiting).

ideas.title: [JACK] 2nd Street — Osaka
ideas.notes: Multiple locations in Osaka. Amerikamura and Shinsaibashi area branches are best. Same chain as Tokyo but different stock — worth checking. Good for mid-range vintage fashion and Japanese-label finds.

ideas.title: [JACK] Super Bookoff — Osaka
ideas.notes: Large Bookoff locations in Namba and Shinsaibashi. Good for retro games, manga, vintage clothing. Tax-free over ¥5,000 with passport.

ideas.title: [JACK] Onegi-chan — pickles
ideas.notes: Recommended by Jack for Japanese pickles (tsukemono). Could be a market stall, specialist pickle shop or konbini find. Exact location unclear — ask Jack or search Tabelog closer to the trip.

JR Special Rapid (新快速) from Osaka Station Platform 4–7

Himeji is visible from the train on the right as you pull into the station — the castle is that close.

items.notes: Straight 15 min walk north from station — you can see it from the exit.

items.detail:
Himeji Station North Exit → Castle: Straight 1km walk north along Otemae-dori.
You can see the white castle at the end of the boulevard from the moment you exit the station.
The walk takes ~15 min. Castle entrance is at the south base of the hill.

items.notes: UNESCO World Heritage. Japan's finest surviving feudal castle. ¥2,400pp. Allow 2 hours. Steep narrow stairs.
items.address: Himeji Castle, 68 Honmachi, Himeji, Hyogo 670-0012, Japan
items.detail:
Himeji Castle (姫路城) — Shirasagi-jo (White Egret Castle)
Hours: 09:00–17:00 (last entry 16:00) · Entry: ¥2,400pp · Combo with Koko-en: ¥3,000pp

Japan's most spectacular surviving feudal castle. UNESCO World Heritage, National Treasure. Remarkably survived earthquakes, fires, WWII bombing raids and 400 years of weather intact.

Structure: Main donjon is 6 storeys visible, 7 inside. Steep narrow staircases — hold the rope rail.

Defensive features: Boiling water holes, gun and arrow ports shaped asymmetrically to confuse enemies, a maze-like approach to slow attackers.

Top floor: Small Shinto shrine. Views across Himeji city.

items.notes: 9 connected traditional Japanese gardens. ¥1,000pp (combo ticket with castle ¥3,000pp saves ¥400pp).
items.address: Himeji Castle, 68 Honmachi, Himeji, Hyogo 670-0012, Japan
items.detail:
Koko-en Garden (好古園)
Entry: ¥1,000pp standalone · ¥3,000pp combo with castle · Hours: 09:00–18:00
Access: Directly adjacent to the castle, west moat side

Nine connected traditional Japanese gardens on the former site of Himeji domain's outer samurai residences. Built 1992 in authentic Edo period garden styles.

Highlights: Garden of the Residence (large stroll garden), Pine Tree Garden, Bamboo Grove, Tea Garden.
Much quieter than the castle itself.

items.notes: Anago (sea eel) and locally grown rice are Himeji specialities.
items.address: Himeji Castle, 68 Honmachi, Himeji, Hyogo 670-0012, Japan
items.detail:
Lunch in Himeji — local specialities:
Anago (穴子 — conger sea eel): Himeji's defining local dish. Lighter and more delicate than unagi (freshwater eel). Restaurants along Otemon-dori specialise in anago rice bowls (anago-meshi). Budget ¥1,500–2,500pp.

Himeji ramen: Mild soy base, thin noodles — regional style worth trying.

items.notes: Direct, 60 min. · ¥1,520pp (buy at JR vending machine — no reservation needed)
items.address: Himeji Station, 68 Ekimae-cho, Himeji, Hyogo 670-0927, Japan

items.notes: Tiny atmospheric alley with moss-covered Fudo statue, candlelit restaurants.
items.address: Hozenji Yokocho, Chuo Ward, Osaka 542-0076, Japan
items.detail:
Hozenji Yokocho (法善寺横丁)
Access: 2 min walk from Dotonbori east end · Only 80m long

The centrepiece: Fudo Myo-o stone statue completely covered in thick moss — growing for over 60 years from visitors continuously ladling water over it as offerings. At night with candlelight, one of Osaka's most remarkable sights.

The alley: Both sides lined with traditional wooden restaurant fronts, paper lanterns. Complete contrast with the neon of Dotonbori just steps away.

items.notes: Last full evening in Japan. Konbini snacks, KitKat flavours. Pack tonight.
items.address: Shinsaibashi, Chuo Ward, Osaka 542-0085, Japan
items.detail:
Last shopping evening in Japan.

Final purchases:
- Konbini haul: 7-Eleven or Lawson — Pocky, regional KitKat flavours (matcha, sake, wasabi, sakura), Calbee crisps, unusual snacks
- Don Quijote Namba: Open until 05:00. Last-minute electronics accessories, cosmetics, food gifts, unusual items
- Matsumoto Kiyoshi: Japanese skincare, health products
- Souvenir shops: Osaka-specific gifts — takoyaki plates, Biliken figures, Glico Running Man merchandise

Tip: Pack tonight — early start tomorrow.

items.notes: ⚠ BOOK AHEAD. Osaka is Japan's food capital — make this one count.
items.address: Candeo Hotels Osaka The Tower, 5-2-28 Shibata, Kita-ku, Osaka 530-0012, Japan
items.detail:
Final dinner in Japan — make it exceptional.

Osaka is the right city for a memorable last dinner. Options:

Kobe beef experience: Certified Kobe beef restaurant. ¥8,000–15,000pp. Look for the official Kobe beef certification mark.

High-end sushi (omakase): Counter omakase with a skilled chef making each piece in front of you. ¥8,000–15,000pp. Book well ahead.

Farewell izakaya crawl: 2–3 excellent izakayas in Dotonbori, each for 1–2 dishes and a drink. Total ~¥4,000pp but memorable.

Book via hotel concierge or Tablecheck.
